summary: Shirk is the association of partners, peers, or rivals with God (Allah) in His lordship, worship, or attributes. In Islamic theology it is the conceptual and moral opposite of tawhid, the affirmation of divine oneness.
description:
The term shirk derives from an Arabic root meaning "to share" or "to make a partner," and denotes ascribing a sharer (sharīk) to God in that which is proper to Him alone. The Qur'an returns to the theme repeatedly, insisting that God has no partner and that He does not share His powers or the worship due to Him; two verses in the same sura state that God does not forgive that partners be associated with Him while forgiving what is less than that for whom He wills (Q 4:48, which adds that whoever associates partners with God has fabricated a tremendous sin, and Q 4:116, which adds that such a person has gone far astray).

In the dogmatic vocabulary that developed in the classical period, shirk functions as the antonym of tawhid, and much of the doctrine of divine oneness is articulated by negating shirk. Later theological and legal literature commonly distinguished degrees and kinds of shirk, for example a "greater" shirk (al-shirk al-akbar), understood as open association such as directing worship or supplication to another besides God, and a "lesser" or "hidden" shirk (al-shirk al-asghar), covering acts such as performing devotion for the sake of human recognition. Some traditions map these onto the same dimensions used to analyze tawhid, lordship (rubūbiyya), divinity and worship (ulūhiyya), and the divine names and attributes (al-asmāʾ wa-l-ṣifāt).

The precise boundary of what counts as shirk, and whether particular practices fall under it, has been contested among schools and jurists across Islamic history; this entry describes the concept without adjudicating those disputes.
editorial_notes: Scope is deliberately introductory. The greater/lesser (akbar/asghar) taxonomy and the mapping onto rubūbiyya/ulūhiyya/al-asmāʾ-wa-l-ṣifāt are widely attested in classical and later literature but were systematized over time and are stated here descriptively, not as a single agreed schema. The question of which specific practices constitute shirk has been a live and sometimes polemical dispute among schools and movements; per the neutrality policy those disputes are named but not adjudicated, and contemporary sectarian applications of the term are treated as out of scope. Qur'anic references (Q 4:48, 4:116) were checked on 2026-07-18 against the Saheeh International translation (via the Quranic Arabic Corpus, corpus.quran.com): both open with the same clause that God does not forgive that partners be associated with Him but forgives what is less than that for whom He wills; 4:48 closes 'has certainly fabricated a tremendous sin' and 4:116 closes 'has certainly gone far astray'. The description was corrected to reflect those renderings and the earlier 'unverified' self-flag has been removed. A Muslim/Islamic scholarly source has been added for triangulation (neutrality principle #8): al-Shahrastānī's classical heresiography Kitāb al-Milal wa-l-Niḥal, cited at medium confidence as its relevant passages were not read line-by-line. Per neutrality principle #9 the Encyclopaedia of Islam (Gimaret, EI2) and Wikipedia citations were removed. Sourcing note on Hawting (2026-07-18, peer-review follow-up): G. R. Hawting's monograph The Idea of Idolatry and the Emergence of Islam (Cambridge University Press, 1999) was previously mis-cited as the carrier for 'the shirk concept and its typology'. That mis-fit has been corrected: Hawting argues a revisionist thesis about the historical referent of Qurʾanic shirk (that the mushrikūn were most likely monotheists venerating lesser beings, not literal pagan polytheists), and he is now credited only for that historical-referent question, not for the akbar/asghar typology. The akbar/asghar (greater/lesser) distinction and the mapping onto rubūbiyya/ulūhiyya/al-asmāʾ-wa-l-ṣifāt are a later juristic and theological systematization, not a Qurʾanic given; for the typology as such a distinct scholarly carrier was added, Daniel Lav's Salafi Political Theology (Cambridge University Press, 2025), whose chapter on 'Monolatry in Ibn Taymiyya's Theological System' reconstructs exactly this rubūbiyya/ulūhiyya framing, cited at medium confidence as its relevant passages were not read line-by-line. Third-lens pass (2026-07-18): a modern/contemporary Muslim scholarly source has been added, Sherman A. Jackson's On the Boundaries of Theological Tolerance in Islam (Oxford University Press, 2002), which studies al-Ghazālī's definition of unbelief (kufr) and the boundary of Islam, the doctrinal frame in which shirk marks the limit of belief. The entry now triangulates three lenses: Western academic (Hawting, Winter ed.), the tradition's own classical heresiography (al-Shahrastānī), and a modern Muslim scholar (Jackson), which converge in treating shirk as the boundary-defining opposite of tawhid rather than adjudicating which practices fall under it.

relations.note: Shirk is the direct negation of the oneness affirmed by tawhid.
One analyzed dimension is shirk with respect to the divine names and attributes.
Rejection of shirk is bound up with affirming God's incomparability.

historical_period.note: A central Qur'anic theme from the earliest revelation; elaborated into a technical typology by theologians and jurists in the following centuries.

sources.citation: Winter, T. (ed.), The Cambridge Companion to Classical Islamic Theology. Cambridge University Press, 2008.

al-Shahrastānī, Muḥammad ibn ʿAbd al-Karīm. Kitāb al-Milal wa-l-Niḥal. Partial English trans. A. K. Kazi and J. G. Flynn, Muslim Sects and Divisions, Kegan Paul International, 1984. Classical Muslim heresiography surveying the sects and their doctrines on divine oneness and its associationist opposites (dualists, associators).
sources.license: Proprietary translation; classical Arabic text public domain. Cited for reference; relevant passages not read line-by-line.

sources.citation: Hawting, G. R. The Idea of Idolatry and the Emergence of Islam: From Polemic to History. Cambridge Studies in Islamic Civilization. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1999. Academic monograph advancing a revisionist thesis about the historical referent of the Qurʾanic vocabulary of association (shirk, mushrikūn): that the opponents the Qurʾan calls mushrikūn were most likely monotheists accused of compromising strict monotheism (e.g. by venerating lesser beings as intercessors), not literal polytheists or pagan idolaters. Cited here only for that historical-referent question, not for the later akbar/asghar typology.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference; relevant passages not read line-by-line.

sources.citation: Lav, Daniel. Salafi Political Theology. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2025. Academic monograph whose first chapter, 'Monolatry in Ibn Taymiyya's Theological System', reconstructs the systematization of shirk and its opposite tawḥīd along the axes later summarized as tawḥīd al-rubūbiyya (God as sole creator, which Ibn Taymiyya holds even most unbelievers concede) and tawḥīd al-ulūhiyya (God as sole proper object of worship, the axis on which shirk is charged). Cited as the scholarly carrier for the juristic/theological typology of shirk (including the greater/lesser and rubūbiyya/ulūhiyya distinctions) as a later systematization, at medium confidence as its relevant passages were not read line-by-line.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference; relevant passages not read line-by-line.

sources.citation: Jackson, Sherman A. On the Boundaries of Theological Tolerance in Islam: Abū Ḥāmid al-Ghazālī's Fayṣal al-Tafriqa. Karachi: Oxford University Press, 2002 (Studies in Islamic Philosophy). An academic monograph and annotated translation by a contemporary Muslim scholar, treating al-Ghazālī's criterion for what constitutes unbelief (kufr) and where the boundary of Islam lies, the doctrinal frame within which associating partners with God (shirk) marks the limit of belief. Added as the modern Muslim scholarly lens on shirk as the boundary-marking negation of tawhid; cited at medium confidence as its relevant passages were not read line-by-line.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference; relevant passages not read line-by-line.
